Regardless of the industry that you're in, getting to a that's right is one of the biggest competitive edges to performance in negotiation. It works by not only showing your counterpart they are being heard, but gives them a chance to solidify they feel empathy and understanding from you. Thus taking down the final barrier, allowing you to be heard by them. In more cases than we can count, that's right has even triggered the counterpart giving you your deal. That's right is the signal that you met the goal outlined by Stephen Covey in the seven habits of highly effective people. Of seek first to understand, then be understood. The general problem with negotiation was we thought when we said I understand, it was the best way to let the other side know, hey, I get it. It isn't. I understand is the ineffective negotiator's favorite phrase. Those who use it frequently are mystified as to why their deals fall apart or more likely people stop talking with them altogether. When you initiate with an open and unconditionally positive approach, you begin to trigger the reactions that lead to changing minds. In fact, people naturally have an instinctive urge towards socially constructive behavior. We're hardwired to collaborate. Our ancestors who didn't collaborate died alone in the dark. The more a person feels understood and positively regarded, the more likely that urge for constructive behavior will surface and take hold. So keep that in mind the next time you get into an argument, try to make a sale or negotiate with your boss for a job promotion. The purpose of this strategy? So you don't fight battles you don't have to. The bottom line, tactical empathy saves time. Tactical empathy is an outcome enhancement mechanism, among other things. A summary aimed at that's right is an empathy accelerator. Your words get traction sooner. You renegotiate less and when you do, you fix problems faster. Negotiated deals that are successfully implemented are built on a foundation of mutual respect. Listening to your counterpart. Not being able to show a true understanding for them is a phenomenal recipe for bad negotiations as well as bad relationships in everyday life. Although each party has their own set of objectives, own goals and motivations, socially lubricating yeses and your rights that often get thrown out early in an interaction are not in any way a substitute for real understanding between you and your partner. For real productive negotiations to take place, you have to show your counterpart that you truly comprehend where they're coming from. The best way to accomplish this is to use a summary to trigger that's right. Remember the formula. Paraphrase plus labeling equals summary. The power of getting to that place of understanding is not some simple yes. Understanding is crucial in the art of negotiation. That's right is always better than a yes. So strive for it. Because once your counterpart believes that you can see the world in which they inhabit, you fully understand his or her dreams and feelings is when mental and behavioral change becomes possible.
